name: The Visitor Center
image_prompt: The grand atrium lobby of the Jurassic Park Visitor Center on Isla Nublar, daytime. A massive open two-story interior with cream-colored walls and dark tropical wood trim. A polished stone floor with a round tile mosaic at the center reading 'Jurassic Park' with the T-Rex silhouette logo. Two enormous reconstructed dinosaur skeletons as the centerpiece: a T-Rex and a sauropod posed mid-combat under the high ceiling. A sweeping curved staircase to a mezzanine balcony lined with banners. Tall tropical plants in large ceramic pots. Banners hanging from the ceiling in teal, orange, and cream with stylized dinosaur logos. Glass double doors at the far end showing lush green tropical jungle outside. Soft natural daylight from a skylight. Empty, no people, no figures. Colors: cream white, dark tropical wood, jungle green, Jurassic Park brand teal and orange, polished stone floor. Iconic 1993 Jurassic Park atmosphere.
location: The grand atrium of the Jurassic Park Visitor Center on Isla Nublar, Costa Rica. A multi-story lobby with a polished stone floor, a cafeteria and gift shop on one side, an automated tour ride on the other, and a huge branded banner hanging behind two reconstructed dinosaur skeletons mid-combat. The building smells like new carpet and jungle humidity. The park is not open yet. It is not going to be.
time: Afternoon. A tropical storm is moving in from the east. The lights are still on. The generators are supposed to be.
weather: Sticky tropical heat giving way to wind. Palm fronds outside the glass doors beginning to lash. The rain has not started yet but the pressure has dropped. Inside the atrium the AC is still fighting.

atmosphere.lighting: Wide natural daylight through the atrium skylight, cooling to gray-green as the storm comes in. Warm fluorescent tubes in the gift shop cast a faint orange on the nearest banners. The cafeteria is dark; somebody turned off the overhead track lights and only the glass deli cases still glow. The staircase up to the control room is lit by a single sconce.
atmosphere.sound: The low thrum of distant generators. The occasional drip of a leak nobody noticed. The wind starting to push at the front glass. The automated tour ride recording starting and stopping on a glitch loop somewhere in the adjacent wing, speaking the park's welcome in John Hammond's voice. A bird that is not a bird calling from the jungle outside.
atmosphere.crowd: The building was designed for thousands and is empty of all but the working group. Footsteps echo off the stone floor and the dinosaur skeletons. The cafeteria chairs are stacked on tables. The gift shop tills are unmanned.
atmosphere.smell: New carpet, fresh paint, chlorine from somewhere, tropical humidity that the air conditioning has not fully defeated, a faint warm plastic from the banners, the sugar-and-grease ghost of a cafeteria that served lunch once and will not serve it again

layout.rotunda_floor: The main atrium, stone floor inset with a brass-and-tile mosaic of the Jurassic Park logo. Two dinosaur skeletons posed above: a tyrannosaur rearing back from a sauropod's tail strike, frozen mid-fight. Banners for the park's grand opening hung from the ceiling but one corner has come loose and sways whenever the front door opens.
layout.gift_shop: A glass-walled retail space off the rotunda, stocked wall to wall with plush dinosaurs, souvenir mugs, branded hats, and books with the logo embossed in gold. A rack of stuffed raptors is nearest the door. None of them have been sold.
layout.cafeteria: A serving line of glass sneeze guards under dim track lights. Trays of jello, banana cream pie, and sandwiches sit behind the glass, untouched. A long dining area with upended chairs on every table except one, which has been set for four. One of the place settings has been used. Dessert was not finished.
layout.mezzanine_balcony: A curving staircase leads up to a second-floor balcony that overlooks the rotunda. From here the skeletons are at eye level. Doors behind the balcony lead into the control room and the admin offices. The view through the atrium glass is of the jungle road and the storm.
layout.front_veranda: Through the double glass doors, a covered stone veranda with tropical plants in clay pots, looking out at the access road, the empty tour vehicles, and the tree line. The jungle starts about fifty yards out. It does not seem that far.

objects_of_note: The two reconstructed skeletons above the rotunda, T-Rex and sauropod, frozen in combat. The sauropod's tail is slightly out of alignment. Nobody has noticed.
A banner along the mezzanine rail reading 'WHEN DINOSAURS RULED THE EARTH,' one corner coming loose, flapping each time the pressure shifts.
An automated tour vehicle, electric, in the porte cochere outside the front door, its doors standing open and its dashboard warning light blinking amber.
A cafeteria table set for four, one place used, the napkin balled on the plate, a half-eaten bowl of ice cream that has melted into soup.
A jeep with the Jurassic Park logo parked under the front overhang, keys in the ignition, driver's door open. A walkie-talkie on the seat clicks every few minutes with static and then stops.
mood: Eerie and expectant. The building was designed to hold a crowd and does not know what to do with its emptiness. The skeletons above the rotunda were assembled to remind guests that extinction happened. The storm outside is going to happen. The power is going to fail. The working group has the best-equipped facility on the island and the most limited amount of time. Everything inside the building still believes the park is going to open. Nothing outside the building does. Frameworks brought in here will have to reckon with a very simple fact: the fences, somewhere, are already down.
